title: AI Can Boost Critical Thinking — But Only If You Use It at the Right Time
summary: When you're stuck on a tough problem, reaching for an AI chatbot feels like a no-brainer. But new research suggests that timing is everything. In a controlled experiment, computer scientist Mina Lee and colleagues at the University of Chicago assigned 393 participants to write an essay arguing for or against a company's water contamination proposal. They were split into groups based on time (30 minutes vs. 10 minutes) and AI access: early, continuous, late, or none. The results, presented at the 2026 CHI conference in Barcelona, revealed a clear trade-off. Participants with ample time who consulted the chatbot only after working on the problem independently scored highest on essay quality and showed the least "myside bias" — meaning they incorporated more diverse perspectives. Those who never used AI, however, retained the most factual information from the source documents. Under tight deadlines, the pattern flipped: early AI access led to the best essay scores, but at a cost. "When you are under time pressure and use AI to boost your performance, then you are basically risking just taking and using the AI's framing," Lee explains. "That reduces the kinds of arguments that you make and your engagement with the documents." The findings align with dual-process theories of learning. Slow, effortful reasoning builds deep understanding; fast, automatic thinking relies on shortcuts. By engaging with material before turning to AI, participants activated that slower, more deliberate mode. Barbara Oakley, an education expert at Oakland University, notes that this mirrors how we learn best: grappling with a problem first, then seeking help. Lee hopes the study encourages AI literacy — knowing not just how to use chatbots, but when. "I think our work kind of targets time constraints as the first step towards that understanding," she says. In a world where AI is increasingly embedded in work and school, the lesson is clear: the smartest users aren't those who offload thinking fastest, but those who know when to think for themselves.
why_it_matters: As AI becomes a permanent fixture in classrooms and workplaces, the line between tool and crutch will blur. Knowing how to time AI use could determine whether a generation develops deep reasoning skills or becomes dependent on bots to think for them.

keywords.explanation: The tendency to favor evidence that supports one's own viewpoint and ignore opposing arguments.
Psychological models that describe two systems of thinking: fast, automatic, and slow, deliberate reasoning.
The ability to use AI tools effectively, including knowing when it is helpful and when it might undermine thinking.
Limits on how long someone has to complete a task, which affect how deeply they can think.
A research design where variables are carefully manipulated to isolate cause-and-effect relationships.
The particular way information or arguments are structured, which can shape how people understand an issue.

questions.question: Who led the experiment on AI timing and critical thinking?
At which conference were the results presented?
Under ample time, which group produced the highest essay quality and least bias?
options: Those who used AI from the start
Those who consulted AI only after working independently
questions.correct_answer: Those who consulted AI only after working independently

questions.question: What is 'myside bias' as used in the article?
options: Favoring information that aligns with one's own viewpoint
The tendency to write too many arguments
questions.correct_answer: Favoring information that aligns with one's own viewpoint

questions.question: According to the article, what theory explains why late AI use works better?
What does Mina Lee hope the study encourages?
options: AI literacy — knowing when to use AI
questions.correct_answer: AI literacy — knowing when to use AI

background_read: The tension between quick shortcuts and deep thinking is not new. Psychologist Daniel Kahneman (the Nobel laureate who wrote 'Thinking, Fast and Slow') popularized dual-process theory, describing System 1 (fast, intuitive) and System 2 (slow, deliberate). In education, Barbara Oakley (an engineering professor and author of 'A Mind for Numbers') has long emphasized that struggling with a problem before seeing the solution creates stronger neural connections.

AI chatbots like ChatGPT have become homework assistants for millions of students, raising fears of intellectual laziness. Previous studies showed that when people offload thinking too early, they miss out on building their own mental models. This experiment is one of the first to rigorously test the *timing* of AI use under realistic writing conditions.

The study was presented at the 2026 CHI conference (the flagship annual conference on Human-Computer Interaction, running since 1982) in Barcelona. It involved 393 participants who had to argue for or against a company accused of water contamination, a topic that required synthesizing multiple documents — a common school and workplace task.

The findings underscore that AI literacy must go beyond knowing how to prompt a bot. Just as we learn to evaluate sources in a library, students need to learn *when* to bring AI into the process. Used too early, AI's framing can overshadow independent reasoning; used strategically, it becomes a powerful revision partner.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Using AI at the right moment can boost critical thinking; using it too soon may backfire.
KEY FINDING: In a controlled experiment with essay-writing, timing of AI access changed outcomes.
├─ SETUP: 393 participants, two time conditions (30 vs 10 min), four AI groups (none, early, late, continuous)
├─ RESULT 1: Under ample time, late AI use produced highest essay quality and least 'myside bias'
└─ RESULT 2: Under time pressure, early AI improved performance but led to adopting the AI's framing and reduced engagement
EXPLANATION: Aligns with dual-process theories — slow, effortful reasoning before AI activates deeper thinking.
├─ QUOTE: Lee says time pressure risks 'just taking and using the AI's framing'
└─ EDUCATION EXPERT: Barbara Oakley notes that grappling first mirrors optimal learning.
IMPLICATION: AI literacy requires knowing when to use AI, not just how.
OPEN QUESTION: How to design AI tools that encourage independent thinking and better timing?

perspectives.description: This research validates a balanced approach: AI can be a powerful revision assistant that sharpens arguments and supplies diverse perspectives. When students first wrestle with material on their own, then use AI, they produce richer essays and retain more ownership of their reasoning.

The short-time condition reveals a dark side: under deadline pressure, people reflexively adopt AI's framing, reducing their own engagement. This could lead to a generation of learners who echo chatbots rather than cultivate original thought, especially in high-pressure testing environments.

The results neatly map onto dual-process theories. AI effectively plugs into the fast, automatic 'System 1' mode when used early, providing ready-made structure but bypassing the slow, effortful 'System 2' that builds understanding. The key variable isn't AI access, but whether the user has already activated deep processing.

Future AI interfaces could enforce a 'think-first' delay or prompt users to draft main points before chatting. Schools might incorporate AI literacy curricula that explicitly teach strategic timing, much like they now teach source evaluation. As this becomes a norm, the distinction between using AI and being used by it will become central to digital citizenship.
